questions.question: Select all that are true about the leaderboard as Sara describes it.
choices: It bundles a set of changes and runs a lightweight training run to get signal on whether they improve the model.
It is a lightweight probe rather than a full production training run.
It is a public ranking of already-released models.
It is the serving stack that hosts the model for inference.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The leaderboard is the change-bundling plus lightweight-probe experiment process, not a public ranking or the serving stack.

questions.question: Select all that are true about what a data run changes.
choices: It changes the architecture while holding the training data fixed.
It changes both the data and the architecture at the same time.
It changes only the tokenizer.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A data run changes only the training data and holds the architecture fixed, so none of the three listed descriptions is correct.

questions.question: Select all that are true about why the team changes only one lever per run.
choices: Isolating one change makes any metric movement attributable to a single cause.
Changing two levers at once makes a metric move impossible to attribute cleanly.
The main reason is that changing two things at once is impossible to implement in code.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Isolation is about attribution: one lever keeps the result attributable, and two levers make a metric move unattributable. It is not about coding difficulty.

questions.question: Select all that are true about a compliant architecture.
choices: It is a Gemma architecture with proprietary Gemini pieces stripped or rewritten so it can be open-sourced.
It simply means an architecture that has passed all safety evals.
It is the Hugging Face serving implementation itself.
It is a quantized build produced for local devices.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Compliant means the proprietary Gemini material is removed or rewritten so Gemma can be open-sourced. It is not a safety-eval pass, the serving code, or a quantized build.

questions.question: Select all that are true about Gemma 4's roughly 10 percent tool-call pass rate at launch.
choices: It was caused by the chat template and formatting, not by the weights.
It was fixed by template updates rather than by retraining the weights.
It was caused by the model having too few parameters.
It was caused by a broken optimizer during training.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The low pass rate was all template and formatting, fixed by template updates. The weights themselves were fine, so parameter count and optimizer are not the cause.

questions.question: Select all that are true about confirming an open model is faithful once a compliant architecture is ready.
choices: A parity check compares the Hugging Face implementation against the internal model.
Parity means the open implementation reproduces the internal model within tolerance.
Faithfulness is confirmed by running a larger leaderboard experiment.
Faithfulness is confirmed by training a new tokenizer.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A parity check reproduces the internal model with the open HF/PyTorch implementation within tolerance. A bigger leaderboard run or a new tokenizer does not establish faithfulness.

questions.question: Select all that are true about why the team runs evals as reproducible infrastructure (Terraform and Cloud Run).
choices: It gives reproducible, identical environments across runs.
It shows diffs before apply and rolls back automatically on failure.
It turns a brittle 15-page setup document into code.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Reproducible environments, safe diffs with rollback, and codifying tribal setup knowledge are all infrastructure-as-code benefits the team highlighted.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about why academic single-turn evals missed the tool-call regression.
choices: They exercise multi-turn tool-calling, so they should have caught the regression.
The regression stayed invisible because they never exercise multi-turn or tool-calling.
They missed it because they were accidentally run on the wrong model.
They missed it because they only measure inference speed.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Only the second statement is true: single-turn evals stay high because they do not exercise multi-turn or tool-calling. The other three are false, so they are the NOT-true selections.
